data.title: LQDW: Buy The Premium, Not The Rate Rally
data.description:
The iShares Investment Grade Corporate Bond BuyWrite Strategy ETF (LQDW) is a derivative income-focused ETF. Every month, LQDW will sell a covered call over LQD, collecting option premium as income. The premium gets distributed to shareholders alongside the coupons of the underlying bonds, which boosts the headline yield above what LQD alone would pay, but also caps any price appreciation in LQDW above that month’s strike price. This ETF is really meant for specific regimes but is marketed as an ETF for all market conditions. The mismatch between what LQDW is and how it is shown to retail investors causes most of the confusion in this fixed income space. Understanding a regime map and where this type of ETF excels is the entire thesis. With correct use, this ETF is a good buy, but in the wrong environment, an investor is systematically selling bond rallies for option premiums that will not compensate for the difference. For this reason, I am initiating LQDW as a buy on Seeking Alpha. For the current rate environment, I believe LQDW is favorable, but I will be somewhat cautious as we head into the end of this year.

We are currently in the only rate environment where LQDW’s structure delivers favorably, which is a range-bound rate environment. There is now only one rate cut priced for late 2026, at the earliest, which is down from around 3 heading into 2026. Additionally, due to elevated inflation uncertainty and geopolitical fronts, the Fed is in a deliberate pause at this time. The March 2026 FOMC held the fed funds rate at 3.50%-3.75%, revised core PCE expectations upward to 2.7%, and acknowledged the Iran-U.S.-Israel conflict, citing energy shocks of some size and duration. Additionally, the futures market also implies a 48% chance that there is no rate cut at all in 2026, which is up from 30% (before the conflict continued to escalate). In addition, the CME FedWatch tool also prices one small 25 bps cut likely in late Q3 or early Q4, and JPM Research doesn’t expect one at all.

In an environment as described above, LQD doesn’t go anywhere meaningful. When LQD remains flat, LQDW’s monthly covered call now imposes zero opportunity cost. The fund collects the coupon income from the underlying IG debt, plus option income. Given the expected rate environment, I am initiating the buy on LQDW, for now, with a reassessment towards the end of the year to see where the economy and the rate environment are at.

When Buy-Write Works (and When it Doesn’t)

Bond-based covered call ETFs have performance conditional on the regime they are working in. There are three rate environments, and LQDW has a distinct return profile in each of them.

In a rate sell-off, such as 2022-2023, the covered call writing effectively provides 25-35 bps of monthly premium against an instrument with a derivative duration of approximately 8.5 years. A 300+ bps move can potentially generate a 25%+ NAV decay that no option premium can compensate for. The 2.51% annualized return since inception is an unfortunate monument to launching the fund three years before one of the most aggressive rate-hike cycles in the history of the modern Fed. It isn’t that the fund is terrible; it was just deployed at the worst time, in the worst possible regime for this type of instrument.

In a rally of rates, covered call writing becomes a drag on return. When the NAV of LQD rises (as rates fall or credit spreads compress), LQDW doesn’t capture anything above the monthly strike price. The written call is then exercised, and the price of appreciation belongs to the buyers of the call. During the previous rate cuts in 2024, investors who held through the easing cycle collected the options premium but missed the rate rally entirely. This is another scenario that makes LQDW unappealing, and it is the scenario a multi-cut easing cycle would recreate. This is also why equity covered call ETFs such as JEPQ aren’t completely comparable, as implied volatility (IV) of equity is materially higher than IV of bonds, which generates a higher premium (due to options pricing). LQDW cannot replicate the equity covered call income dynamic, as the IV is mathematically lower, and does not support it.

In range-bound or sticky rate regimes, the covered call cap is irrelevant. LQD remains flat, and the monthly written call expires worthless, which allows the fund to collect the coupon of the underlying, in addition to the full premium of the covered call, with zero lost opportunity cost. This is the only regime in which the ETF delivers its design. The forward FOMC pathway, geopolitical conflict-fueled energy inflation, and the Fed's cautious wait-and-see approach together make this the base case through at least Q3 2026. This shorter time horizon also makes NAV decay negligible.

The core problem of marketing this type of product is that these products, especially within the fixed income asset class, are complex and work differently than equities do. A headline distribution rate of 8-17% attracts yield chasers who do not see the difference between earned income, option premium, and, notably, return of capital. Those who hold the fund through rate regime changes that are hostile to the strategy end up eroding their principal. The 30-day SEC yield of 4.35% is a more honest number for the bond portfolio, with anything above being the option premium, which is still earned income, but MOVE-dependent, conditional on the environment, and bounded by implied volatility of bonds in a way that equity covered call funds are not.

Mechanics and Premium Economics

LQDW attempts to track the CBOE LQD BuyWrite Index (BXLB), which holds LQD and writes a euro-style, 1MTE, ATM call each month, with an XP at the listed option closest to 100% of LQD’s prior-day price at the close. The option is priced at the theoretical euro bid at 2 PM EST on the roll date, with the premium proceeds functionally reinvested.

Meanwhile, the income dynamics rely largely on the MOVE index, which is the bond market equivalent of VIX. Higher MOVE means higher IV, and higher IV means higher premiums collected on call writing (for treasury-linked instruments). At MOVE levels 50-70, the premium is decent, but with MOVE at 80-95 (current), premiums can run from 28-38 bps per month. If MOVE ascends past 100, premiums can reach over 50 bps a month, which can push annualized income towards 10% and beyond.

MOVE is approximately 82, within a 52-week range of 56 and 140. This is the main conflict point to the LQDW buy, as the range-bound environment justifies participating in an options strategy that revolves around a flat derivative, and is also suppressing the volatility that allows option writers to reap premiums from writing. A rating upgrade would be warranted if MOVE surged past 100, as the premium collection would be more compelling. The geopolitical background, currently involving Iran, tariff policy, and fiscal dynamics, provides a pathway to an elevated MOVE as well.

Analysis of the Underlying

Spread decomposition on the underlying IG portfolio:

Component Contribution
10-year Treasury (benchmark) ~4.25%
IG credit OAS (~95–100 bps, ICE BofA IG Index) ~0.95–1.00%
Liquidity discount ~0.10%
Expense ratio -0.34%
Net SEC yield ~4.35%
Option premium (est., MOVE ~82) ~3.4–4.2% ann.
Total gross distribution ~7.8–8.5%

IG credit spreads remain near the tighter end of their historical range at approximately 90–105 bps OAS. Tight spreads are not beneficial, as there is less of a ballast against deterioration, but they are not an end-all, be-all in an environment where credit fundamentals are relatively stable, and the base case is not a recession. In the 2026 credit outlook by Schwab, IG yields near the upper end of the 15-year range remain compelling for income-oriented portfolios even with tight spreads.

The effective duration of approx. 8.5 years creates meaningful sensitivity to rate changes, as a 50 bps shift can generate a 4.25% NAV impact. On a 3-6 month time horizon with range-bound rates as a base case, the exposure is acceptable to me. Six months of premium income at the current rate of 3.75%-4% annually (or about 2.0% over 6 months) provides a meaningful cushion against a modest rate move before the position is underwater. The tail risk is an interest rate shock similar to what was seen in 2022, which the current macro environment argues against at this time, but can never be fully ruled out in a geopolitically tense environment.

Peer Comparison

Metric LQDW TLTW HYGW LQD JAAA
Underlying LQD (IG Corps) TLT (20+ Tsy) HYG (HY Corps) iBoxx IG AAA CLOs
Strike ATM 2% OTM ATM None None
Expense Ratio 0.34% 0.35% 0.69% 0.15% 0.22%
30-Day SEC Yield 4.35% ~3.8% ~5.5% ~4.4% ~6.5%
Max Drawdown -9.2% -18.6% -5.5% -24.9% -0.6%
Range-bound fit High High Moderate Low High
Easing-cycle fit Low Moderate Moderate High High

In a range-bound rate environment, LQDW and TLTW are better positioned than what their inception date returns suggest, as both were launched at arguably the worst possible time for having exposure to duration. The relevant comparison here is not historical performance, but the forward fits relative to the Fed's glidepath. As I cover more fixed income articles, duration risk is the real sticking point at this time. And with duration risk in mind, the only clear outperformer in our comparison is once again JAAA, as floating-rate instruments essentially zero out duration risk. But, while JAAA seems to belong in most income-oriented rate sleeves regardless of the rate regime, LQDW has a nice spot specifically in the range-bound window for purposes of diversification and flexibility when dealing with tighter IG corporate spreads.

Tradability

LQDW trades on CBOE BZX and has approximately 59,000 shares of average daily volume. Bid-ask spreads run between 1 and 2 cents, which are adequate for both retail and institutional investors. LQDW has a beta of 0.30 and has a 0.83 correlation with LQD, which is in line with the bond-correlated profile and in line with a lower volatility reading.

As far as NAV, temporary NAV pressure from brief rate spikes tends to recover from the subsequent premium harvesting, which further helps tradability.

Downside Risks

The main downside risk once again falls to the conversation surrounding duration at this time. The duration risk is unhedged. 8.5-year duration creates a 4.25% NAV sensitivity per 50 bps rate move. A surprise hawkish stance, with energy inflation as the most likely catalyst, is the primary tail risk involving LQDW. It is worth noting that this is an explicit risk flagged by the Fed in the March FOMC statement. Additionally, if inflation remains resilient and in line with falling to the Fed's 2.0% target, rate cuts could resume a steady cadence, which would cause additional pain for LQDW holders in the form of capped gains on a fixed income rally, for longer duration bonds (such as the ones in LQD). This scenario captures the rate rally regime and the capped returns due to covered call writing.

Takeaway

I rate LQDW as a buy, for now, on the basis that both I and institutions largely expect minimal rate cuts for the rest of the year, with yields remaining relatively flat due to inflationary consequences of sustained crude prices exceeding $100 BOE. The thesis is specific to our current rate environment, and if the rate environment changes, so does the thesis. An upgrade to “strong buy” would involve a sustained MOVE of 100 or more, and a downgrade to sell would involve a multi-cut Fed pivot, or a credit event widening OAS past 75 bps. Neither is the base case today, so a light buy is a good point for LQDW to sit at.

data.title: CVS Health: A Diversified Income Play With Improving MA Rate Visibility For 2027
data.description:
CVS Health Overview

CVS Health's stock (CVS) was in turmoil upon my last check following the CMS's "flat" proposal change for 2027.

In January, the CMS issued an Advance Notice that proposed a near-flat 0.09% net average Medicare Advantage, or MA, payment increase for 2027. Given rising medical costs/claims, this was a disastrous proposal for MA-associated companies.

Fortunately for CVS, as you can see for yourself when driving past some street corners, its business is diversified (i.e., its high-margin Pharmacy & Consumer Wellness segments), its stock isn't "expensive," and CVS's dividend yield offers solace for income-focused investors.

Things have since brightened after it was announced that the Trump administration "will increase average MA payments by 2.48%, or more than $13B, in 2027."

Below, I revisit CVS (in spirit) to see how the payment change impacts its business.

Recent Developments

The 2.48% is "bittersweet" because it still falls below the 5.06% finalized for 2026. But, as my mother used to tell me as a child, "It's better than nothing!"

Before I go any further, it's important to contextualize CVS's dependence on Medicare-related businesses (via its Aetna/Health Care Benefits segment).

CVS generated $402.1B in total enterprise revenue last year (up 7.8% YoY). Government premiums (predominantly from MA + Part D) made up $103.4B of that, or ~26% of total company revenue.

This also represents 72% of CVS's Health Care Benefits segment ($143.4B total revenue).

So, while this is, for sure, a material amount, it is a moderate exposure compared to "pure-play" MA insurers.

All together, CVS's Health Care Benefits segment generated $2.9B in adjusted operating income in 2025.

So, how exactly do rate changes impact the full causal chain?

The table above assumes stable membership, a rate applied to the full government block, and flat medical costs.

The CMS payment rate is added directly to the denominator (premium revenue). And higher rates increase total premiums collected. Assuming medical and quality improvement costs are unchanged, Medical Benefit Ratio, or MBR, drops.

"MBR" tells us how much of every premium dollar is left after medical claims. The remaining "gross margin" covers admin/other expenses and flows into adjusted operating income.

And, as I've alluded to in my previous MA-related analyses, even tiny MBR adjustments swing hundreds of millions to billions in profit.

As I noted in my discounted cash flow analysis on CVS from February, CVS's valuation appears 8.5x more sensitive to changes in margin than revenue growth.

The final rate increase adds roughly $2.56B in incremental 2027 revenue. The lift directly addresses the variable that has historically limited CVS the most.

Now, many insurers, CVS included, have been modestly pulling back on MA plans for 2026. This is in an effort to prioritize margins over volume.

CVS, in particular, reduced its MA footprint by ~4% in membership and geographic reach. This is actually less aggressive than peers like UnitedHealth (UNH) and Humana (HUM).

Now, the final CMS rate boost came after these 2026 decisions were locked in. So it may limit further pullbacks for 2027.

Dividend Safety

What does this all mean for CVS's investment thesis?

For one, CVS literally makes "pennies on the dollar." This is why margin changes are much more impactful on valuation than growth.

Right now, CVS's "Interest Coverage" is approximately 2.97x (8-quarter trailing average EBIT / interest expense). Ideally, this is higher (>3.0). Of course, companies are obligated to pay debt before giving back to shareholders. So, more cash generation = more interest coverage = dividend safety/share buybacks.

For CVS, dividend safety is quite important. The dividend is "non-negotiable" for income investors.

CVS's annual dividend is currently $2.66/share, or roughly $3.38B in total cash outflow (1.27B shares outstanding). In FY25, operating cash flow and free cash flow were $10.6B and $7.8B, respectively. And recall that management guided for "at least $9.0B" in OCF in FY26. So, on cash flow, only ~32% of OCF or ~43% of FCF payout ratios look pretty safe.

As I noted in February, CVS pays ~$3B in annual interest expense. So better cash flow visibility could also accelerate deleveraging (debt-to-equity is already low at 1.12x) or opportunistic buybacks (should shares stay attractive).

Valuation

Perhaps as a homage to its business diversification, CVS has outperformed its peers in the last year.

Despite this, CVS's valuation remains relatively cheap.

Now, this is, in part, because CVS has the lowest net-income margin (0.44%) and ROE (2.35%) in the group. But cash flow (1.48% levered FCF margin) holds up better than the P&L suggests.

With its stock pretty much unchanged since my February analysis, the valuation assumptions that I brought up remain (2.4% Revenue Growth and 1.9% FCF Margin).

Conclusion

In my last analysis, I concluded that "CVS's diversity also provides a buffer against negative MA-related developments, making it a 'safer' option for income investors who want exposure to 'beaten-down' insurers," and this remains true to this day. In fact, CVS's new Health 100 platform + Google (GOOGL) partnership (announced earlier this month) further supports its diversification efforts. This will create an open engagement engine that can be later licensed to other PBMs/pharmacies/payers. What's "special" about CVS is that its 90M addressable shoppers can become a competitive moat and lead to high-margin growth opportunities if deployed wisely.

That said, the 2.48% rate boost doesn't reduce utilization risk. Nor does it make CVS a high-growth story. But it does improve the margin trajectory that I highlighted as the dominant DCF driver. I think the rate change is properly accounted for with today's ~7% intraday price rally (maintain "Hold").

As for a potential future re-rating on CVS, a beat and raised full-year guide would be an obvious "Buy" signal. A 2026/2027 OCF guidance raise, in particular, would de-risk CVS's entire model (support debt paydown and opportunistic buybacks, leading to multiple expansion). I'm also looking for any positive commentary on 2027 bids or Star-rating momentum when CVS next reports Q1 earnings on May 6.

data.title: Ideaya Biosciences: Key Pivotal Data Imminent
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I've been on record as an optimist on the outlook for IDEAYA Biosciences (IDYA) starting around last year, due in large part to the untapped potential of their lead compound, darovasertib. As their story continues to evolve, IDYA is continuing to pick up new pipeline projects and push toward a critical readout in what is arguably their most important trial to date. Today, I want to digest recent updates on the progress of this trial and other pipeline progress and continue to make the case for optimism in this stock thesis.

Pipeline Updates

Darovasertib

The main pipeline candidate for IDYA remains the protein kinase C inhibitor darovasertib, being explored as an option for patients with uveal melanoma harboring a specific, very common biomarker. As a reminder, uveal melanoma continues to represent an important unmet need, with no traditional chemotherapy, targeted therapy, or immunotherapy so far being able to really touch it. We have tebentafusp for patients of a certain HLA type, but uveal melanoma remains a mostly unmet need in cancer.

There are two stories unfolding with darovasertib: as part of a combination in metastatic disease and as a single agent in the pre-surgical setting. For the former, we last saw an update on overall survival at the SMR meeting back in October 2025. Median overall survival for patients in this phase 1/2 study was 21.1 months, comparable to what has been seen with tebentafusp in selected patients and beating historical benchmarks that would be expected in this population.

These findings are being further developed in the OptimUM-02 study, which IDYA anticipates will support a filing for approval with the FDA. Topline findings for patients with HLA*A2-negative disease have been guided for the last week of March 2026, but days before this timing, IDYA announced the readout would be delayed until after the database lock in the first half of April, with the data readout not having occurred at the time of writing. The phase 3 portion of this study, which will include 435 patients, has fully enrolled.

The other main throughline for darovasertib is its use as a monotherapy for patients who have not yet had surgery for uveal melanoma. A readout of the phase 2 OptimUM-09 trial was presented at ESMO 2025 back in October, with darovasertib treatment leading to tumor shrinkage in 78 of 94 patients (54% had 20% or higher tumor shrinkage). Over half of the patients in the study who were recommended for enucleation were able to preserve their eyes, and substantial proportions of patients had improved visual acuity with neoadjuvant darovasertib.

A phase 3 trial for neoadjuvant darovasertib is continuing to enroll patients, with IDYA estimating completion of enrollment by the first half of 2027.

Other pipeline projects

In my last article, I went into some of the new forays for targeted therapy that IDYA is continuing to explore. For the sake of brevity, I won't be revisiting each of these in depth, but IDYA has started up or received clearance to start trials for various early-phase trials for antibody-drug conjugates, MTAP pathway inhibitors, and inhibitor against new targets like KAT6/7.

Financial Overview

At the end of 2025, IDYA held $112.8 million in cash and equivalents, along with $526.6 million and $410.2 million in short- and long-term investments, respectively. Collaboration revenue for the year was $218.7 million, while operating expenses reached $378 million (up from $334 million in 2024). After interest, the net loss for the year was $113.7 million.

The net cash used to fund operations for 2025 reached $71.1 million, although this was offset by one-time milestone payments that will be unpredictable in the years to come. If we take those out of the equation, IDYA has an estimated negative cash flow of approximately $290 million.

At this cash burn rate, the company has approximately 3 to 4 years of operational cash runway, which does not take into account future potential milestone payments from their Servier collaboration.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Well capitalized to fund near-term operations

Assuming their data are eventually good enough to reach FDA approvals, IDYA has the cash on hand to support themselves through the submission, review, and initial drug launch periods for darovasertib. Bringing Servier's resources into the mix (at the exchange of ex-US rights to darovasertib sales) makes the runway even more comfortable, assuming no major hiccups are seen in the process.

Strength - A dizzying array of new irons in the fire

This funding runway is fueling a very wide range of compounds that have left the discovery and development phase into clinical trials, and IDYA is poised to capitalize on some very high-interest targets like B7-H3 and MTAP deletion, neither of which have materialized yet but stand poised to become the next "menin-level" targeted therapies of interest in select fields of serious unmet need like small cell lung cancer and pancreatic cancer.

Mixed/Unknown - Big data readout could be make or break

Of course, all eyes should be on this upcoming database lock and readout, which analysts expect will drift into a May timeframe. In my view, it's not a long wait to see about the approvability of this highly promising drug, and IDYA may soon be poised to start carving inroads into a market sized in the billions of dollars in the US alone.

That said, nothing is guaranteed here, and the temptation from the analysis side of this equation is to start trying to read tea leaves about the timeline for database lock and how that "must" translate into a positive or negative readout, since the trial's assessments are "event driven" (i.e., they're timed to occur only after a certain number of patients have progressed or died). Some might argue that the longer the trial takes, the better that bodes for the trial findings.

But this is an exercise in folly, as old shareholders of Glycomimetics can attest, after finding out that patients in the control arm did much better than expected in a pivotal trial. All of that work on the part of armchair experts evaporated. And IDYA's trial is far from guaranteed to be a success.

Strength - Two-pronged approach suggests darovasertib is doing what we expect

One thing I am willing to read the tea leaves on is whether darovasertib is an active agent at all. The trial that's about to read out is focused on patients with metastatic uveal melanoma, and it's being given in combination with crizotinib. We do not know at this time whether there is synergy between the two agents or, indeed, whether crizotinib is carrying the load for the benefit we seem to be seeing.

But the neoadjuvant trial is darovasertib alone, and it appears to be rescuing patients from disfiguring, debilitating surgeries at a very encouraging rate. It gives me a lot of confidence that darovasertib is active in this disease and that we'll have a reasonable chance to see benefit in OptimUM-02.

Risk: Rising costs associated with a spreading pipeline

All of this work is coming at some big costs, with cash burn threatening to climb well over $300 million a year. IDYA may have the liquidity to absorb these costs for quite a while, but this kind of operational outlay puts a lot of pressure on the company to succeed in the clinic and then quickly move to being a big performer in the market, as well.

Bottom-Line Summary

IDYA finds itself on the precipice of a momentous data readout, possibly within the next few weeks. The market ascribes to their pipeline a capitalization of almost $3 billion, which is a sharp premium on their generous liquid holdings. If their anticipated data readout is a success, then darovasertib is all but destined for approval, and IDYA has the potential to be a commercial-stage biotech in 2027.

This valuation is aggressive, yes, but given the backing that they have with Servier, and the potential for approval in a market that has many, many unmet needs, it is likely that a positive readout within this coming month will lead to further improvement in market sentiment. Based on the data we've seen to date, darovasertib is convincingly an active agent, in my opinion, and after watching this for years, I believe this compound will be a game changer for uveal melanoma.

Of course, there are many clinical, regulatory, and operational risks for IDYA. This is far, far from a done deal, but if they can get approval, I think they will be worth more than their current valuation, and they have the funding to get to the point where they can find out what kind of market really exists for darovasertib. Given all this, I continue to remain optimistic about the company, and I maintain a "Buy" sentiment even at these price levels.

data.title: CTS Eventim: Lack Of Visibility Keeps Me On Hold
data.description:
Investment Summary

My recommendation for CTS Eventim AG & Co. KGaA (CEVMF) is a hold rating. In my view, the core ticketing business is still strong, and the venue story is also getting clearer. The issue is that neither point is showing up cleanly in the near-term earnings outlook. FY2026 guidance looks soft, the bridge after the expired long-term contract is not clear, and management did not quantify that impact. So while I still see quality in the assets and some real long-term upside, I do not think there is enough visibility yet to turn bullish.

Business Overview

CEVMF operates through two segments: ticketing and live entertainment. The ticketing segment sells tickets through online platforms, mobile channels, box offices, and partner networks. It also provides software and ticketing solutions to promoters, venues, and other event organizers. The Live Entertainment segment organizes and promotes concerts, tours, festivals, and other live events. It also operates venues such as arenas, theaters, and open-air sites. Basically, CEVMF makes money mainly from ticket sales and service fees, with other revenue streams including software and platform services, event promotion, and venue operations.

4Q25 Earnings Review

In 4Q25, CEVMF reported group revenue of EUR 931 million, a growth of 19% y/y. Growth came from both segments. Ticketing revenue grew 11% y/y to EUR 350 million, while Live Entertainment revenue grew 25% y/y to EUR 595 million. In terms of profitability, Ticketing saw an adj. EBITDA of EUR 196 million, with an adj. EBITDA margin of 55.9%, roughly in line with last year. Live entertainment saw adj. EBITDA of EUR 50 million, with an adj. EBITDA margin of 8.5%. At the group level, adj. EBITDA grew 12% y/y to EUR 246 million, with adj. EBITDA margin at 26.4%. At the bottom line, net income was EUR 128 million, down 7% y/y.

Ticketing Segment

Ticketing is still the best business within CEVMF, in my view. Demand was strong across all quarters in 2025, with each quarter printing y/y growth. Volume-wise, while CEVMF did benefit from the France Billet and See Tickets consolidation for the full year, which helped volume and broadened the geographic footprint, the overall volume strength should not be understated, as CEVMF did sell 177 million tickets in 2025. Now, what really matters here is not that volume grew, but where that growth came from and the implications. Per management disclosure, about two-thirds of retail ticket volume now comes from outside Germany, and I think this is the key takeaway for this segment. This suggests that CEVMF is much less reliant on its legacy core market and is now more of an international platform with multiple local markets. A good comp to think of here is Live Nation Entertainment (LYV), and if CEVMF can show more international traction, the valuation gap between itself and LYV should gradually close (LYV trades at 14.5x NTM EBITDA while CEVMF trades at 5.6x, by the way).

While FY2025 adj. EBITDA margins may look high for this segment at face value; remember that Q4 margin came in at 55.9%, and CEVMF did acquire See Tickets, which is a business of lower margin. Over time, my view is that with See Tickets, CEVMF can offer a broader range of ticketing solutions to B2B partners, be it through the EVENTIM retail platform, access to its customer database, or more services for partners. That would scale up See Tickets' volume, and the incremental margin should be attractive given this is a largely fixed-cost business.

Venues Improve the Upside

Venues are one reason I can see more upside in CEVMF. The main concern is that venues are capital-heavy and tough to scale, but I do not think that framing is right. First of all, management said venue operations already make up ~40% of Live Entertainment EBITDA, with ~46% adj. EBITDA margin, which is way better than the Live Entertainment segment itself. This margin perspective matters a lot for the live entertainment segment, as this is the lower-margin part of the business (5.8% adj. EBITDA margin in FY2025 vs. ticketing at 46.4%). But take note that within live entertainment, there are really two key parts: (1) promoting events and (2) owning and operating venues. They are not the same, and if venue margins are already close to ticketing margins, then as this mix moves up, it should become a very strong earnings driver.

Milan is the best early proof point here. The Unipol Dome opened earlier in 2026 after ~2.5 years of construction, hosted the Olympic ice hockey matches, and is expected to host ~50 shows this year. One key point here is that average ticket prices are running ~10% above the original underwriting. This tells us that this part of the business has room to scale and underlying demand is better than expected. This adds another upside to the story because ticketing has been the main focus of the market for a long time (which is fair given that it is the main adj. EBITDA contributor). But this view should start to change, as you can see from consensus estimates that adj. EBITDA growth is expected to be higher than Ticketing's adj. EBITDA growth over the next two years.

Reasons to Stay Neutral for Now

That said, I am staying neutral because the near-term setup is harder to underwrite. I can see the runway in ticketing, and I can also see why venues could become more important over time. The problem is that neither of those points is showing up cleanly in the 2026 guide. Management guided ticketing KPIs to be at 2025 levels or only slightly above. For live entertainment, they guided revenue to be roughly flat, with only moderate growth in adj. EBITDA. At the group level, the message was the same: that 2026 should be at or only slightly above 2025. While it's fair to say this is against a strong FY2025 performance, nonetheless, it still makes FY2026 look like a year of "meaningful stepdown" from a headline perspective.

Aside from the guide, I am also not a fan of the change tied to the long-term ticketing contract that expired. Management said the new arrangement carries fewer prepayments and now looks more like a regular ticketing contract. I do not think that means demand weakened or that CEVMF's competitive position got worse. But it does tell me part of the previous earnings profile is unlikely to repeat, and more importantly, management did not quantify the impact. This is basically a "void" in the earnings bridge, which means the standard deviation of investors' expectations is going to be wide (this is my view). In this type of situation, the chances of CEVMF missing expectations are elevated, and I try to avoid such potential "miss" stories.

Valuation

I see the potential for CEVMF valuation to go a lot higher from here if the market starts to price this asset like LYV. Given that CEVMF is in a net cash position, if the NTM EBITDA multiple goes up from 5.6x to 14.5x, CEVMF's share price could theoretically be 2.6x from here. The question is when will this re-rating start to happen? If we look at the ratio between CEVMF and LYV multiples, the discount has widened over the past 2 years (sharp de-rating in recent months), and this tells me that the market is still not convinced CEVMF can grow internationally, can continue showing the same earnings strength after the re-pricing of contracts, and that venues are a meaningful earnings growth driver. What CEVMF needs to do here is to show via each quarter that adj. EBITDA can compound at the same rate as LYV over the next few quarters. When that happens, we should start to see an uptick in multiples. Until then, I stay neutral.

Conclusion

I believe CEVMF has the right ingredients in place to become a better story from here. It has a strong ticketing franchise, a more international revenue base, and venue assets that could drive real earnings growth over time. But for now, I think the stock needs more proof. Management has to show that growth can hold up after the contract reset and that venues can indeed drive earnings growth sustainably. Until that happens, I think a hold rating is the right view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Corning: Springboard Plan On Track, Valuations Not - A Hold With A Great Story
data.description:
From the company that brought us Gorilla Glass, Corning (GLW) has had a stellar past year, surging 275% as it underwent a significant earnings inflection through the growth of its optical communications segment. The segment, which now accounts for about 40% of total revenue, has been growing at 35% Y/Y, held up strongly by insatiable AI data center fiber infrastructure demand.

The management has raised "Springboard" targets to $11B in incremental yearly sales by 2028 from the initial $8B target, as it reflects more accurately the durability of AI-driven tailwinds. Positive catalysts include the landmark $6B deal with Meta, a proprietary AI-specific fiber technology, and a nascent optionality in solar. Corning is transitioning from a display/specialty materials company into a high-growth infrastructure play, and while that warrants a premium valuation, we believe the optimism is priced into its forward P/E multiple of 47x. Thus, we think a deeper pullback is needed to make it an attractive opportunity again.

Financial analysis

Corning spent decades mainly as a cyclical display glass company until 2017, when the 5G fiber rollout led its optical communications business to become its main growth driver. Since then, the optical segment has undergone several waves of growth—first, the 5G buildout in 2017-18, then the post-COVID broadband stimulus in 2021-22, and finally the AI data center supercycle, which started in 2024.

We think this wave of optical fiber adoption is far more robust and stickier due to the secular trend of AI infrastructure as opposed to the cyclical telecom capital upgrades of the past. Hence, the stock has massively rerated from mid-teens forward P/E during 2017-18 versus ~20s during COVID and 47x as AI capex heats up.

Segment FY2024 Revenue FY2025 Revenue Y/Y Growth
Optical Communications $4.66B $6.27B +34.7%
Display Technologies $3.87B $3.70B -4.5%
Specialty Materials $2.02B $2.21B +9.5%
Automotive $1.85B ~$1.79B -3%
Life Sciences ~$979M ~$972M -0.7%
Hemlock/Solar $1.10B $1.46B +33.1%

Source: Company, Himalayas Research estimates

Revenue grew at an average of 11.3% from FY2023 to FY2025, while the market expects the company to grow revenue at a faster pace of 17.4% on average during FY2026E to FY2027E. FY2023 was a demand trough as optical revenues fell 20% Y/Y due to inventory digestion at telcos. In FY2024, telco sales normalized while early data center demand emerged, creating an inflection point. Optical manufacturing has high operating leverage, meaning as furnaces and fiber-drawing towers are utilized, costs are relatively stable while incremental revenues directly increase its operating margins.

Metric FY2023 FY2024 FY2025A FY2026E FY2027E
Revenue $12.6B $13.1B $15.6B $18.8B $21.5B
Revenue Growth — +4.2% +19.1% +20.2% +14.2%
Adj. EPS $1.70 $1.96 $2.52 $3.13 $3.92
Adj. EBITDA — $2.49B $3.63B $5.12B $6.10B
Adj. EBITDA Margin — ~17.2% 23.2% ~27.2% ~28.4%
Adj. FCF — $1.25B $1.72B ~$1.97B ~$2.48B

Source: Company, market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates

Net debt increased slightly in FY2025 to $6.1B as the company issued new debt to fund the Hickory, NC, capacity expansion for the Meta contract. Net debt to adjusted EBITDA is 1.7x in FY2025, which is still at comfortable levels.

Adjusted FCF has grown steadily in FY2025 and is expected to continue to increase in FY2026E and FY2027E, helped by strong operating cash flows.

AI fiber technology

Generative AI data centers require significantly more optical fiber than traditional data centers. This structural shift creates a multi-year replacement and buildout cycle that highly benefits the company's optical communications segment. U.S. companies are projected to spend over $1T on AI data centers, with the big tech companies having already spent hundreds of billions in total in 2025. Corning is one of the world's largest optical fiber suppliers and operates one of the most cost efficient production sites in Concord, North Carolina.

Meta's $6B deal with the company, announced in January 2026, evidences Corning's strategic positioning and core strengths in optical fiber. The deal runs through 2030, and will allow Meta to use Corning's fiber to connect its 26 operational or developing U.S. data centers, such as the flagship 5-gigawatt "Hyperion" project.

The Contour fiber platform offers 2x density per conduit (proprietary patent), and the newly announced multi-core fiber system unveiled in March 2026 will have 4x capacity per fiber, 75% fewer connectors, and 60% faster installation. These products command pricing power and steepen switching costs for hyperscaler customers, who are likely locked into specific infrastructure designs. Further, the GlassWorks AI solutions portfolio extends from long-haul fiber to fiber-to-the-chip assemblies. This gives Corning a formidable position across every layer of the AI data center stack.

Springboard Plan gives revenue visibility

The Springboard plan was recently hiked to target $11B in incremental yearly revenue by 2028, providing helpful multi-year revenue visibility. This would imply ~15.5% revenue CAGR from FY2025 to FY2028.

We believe the targets to be realistic because we have the $6B anchored by the Meta deal while we expect similar orders from other hyperscalers. In addition, the company is investing $1.7B in capex in FY2026, which is larger than the annual depreciation. Net new PP&E would come from the Hickory, NC, plant expansion and breaking ground on a new optical connectivity facility in India. This corroborates well with increased customer orders.

Stability with specialty materials

Apple has committed to sourcing all cover glass for iPhones and Apple Watches from Corning's Kentucky plant and has backed it with a $2.5B investment commitment. This provides a structural floor to specialty materials revenue regardless of general smartphone market trends. In FY2025, this segment generated $2.21B in revenue and is broadly stable.

Optionality with solar

Corning's solar market-access platform had 100% of the capacity committed in 2025 and 80% of the capacity secured for the next five years. The company targets $2.5B in revenue by 2028, up from $1.46B in FY2025. This segment grew 33.1% Y/Y in FY2025 and is entirely insulated from trade tariffs as it is domestically manufactured.

This is an often overlooked segment, as it's not part of the core business, but nonetheless could provide upside as the U.S. solar market is expected to grow at low teens in the next five years.

Valuation and verdict

The company is high quality and strategically well positioned—it benefits from the surging demand for optical fibers for AI data centers and is less prone to the cyclicality brought by prior telco capex cycles. The landmark deal with Meta further cements that. It has legacy business lines that are stable, like the cover glass business with Apple and has a solar market-access platform to provide optionality for U.S. domestic solar.

That said, the stock trades at 47x forward P/E for a company growing EPS at ~24% in the next year or ~15.5% annually to 2028. If we use a growth-at-a-reasonable-price (GARP) framework, it would imply a PEG ratio 1.93x, which is more expensive than the typical 1.0x-1.2x range we look for in growth companies. The current valuation multiple is also higher than in previously growth cycles that was around 20x P/E.

While we do believe the forward growth numbers are highly plausible and subject to further upgrades (as the management has done in the past), the current valuation does seems full. We rate GLW a Hold as we wait for a pullback for a better entry.

data.title: IGA: NAV Erosion Likely To Continue (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Voya Global Advantage & Premium Opportunity Fund (IGA), I issued a hold rating due to the valuation at the time. I was concerned about initiating a position while market indices were trading at all-time highs. Since then, the markets have pulled back from those highs, and I wanted to reassess the fund's overall value proposition. IGA provides direct exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world, but the underlying structure makes me a bit cautious about the long-term potential.

When I previously covered IGA, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 5.97%. As the fund continues to pull back over the last few months, the fund now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 7.86%. Although this isn't the deepest discount that the fund has traded at, it's a fairly reasonable valuation for investors looking to accumulate shares at a discount. However, IGA has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.45% over the last five years. Despite the reasonable price to NAV level, I believe there's a chance that the discount to NAV can deepen if market sentiment doesn't improve.

IGA now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 10.7% and issues those payouts on a monthly basis. However, I believe that the funds distribution policy is a bit too generous and is hurting the long-term NAV growth of the fund. If market indices continue to decline through 2026, the fund may be forced to reduce the distribution at some point to slow the rate of NAV erosion. Furthermore, the fund's structure of putting an emphasis on distributions means that the ability for capital appreciation is fairly limited, even through a strong market rally.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, IGA has total net assets of $163.5M that are spread across 244 different holdings. The fund provides direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. As the fund's name implies, IGA provides global exposure. However, I wouldn't necessarily consider IGA to be a dedicated fund since the majority of assets are still allocated to U.S.-focused positions. For instance, IGA still has a 67.4% allocation to U.S. equities, with the remaining portion of the portfolio being spread across different regions of Asia and Europe. The fund takes an actively managed approach, rather than trying to track a particular index.

  1. The fund screens for global stocks with a high yield that is sustainable.
  2. The list is then ranked by valuation metrics to find discounted opportunities.
  3. IGA optimizes its holdings to align with the MSCI World Index.

As a result, IGA tends to lean most heavily towards financials at 26.58% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrial and health care companies, which accounts for 13% and 12.36% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up nearly 52% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors will have the greatest impact on the fund's daily price change and total return potential.

Since the fund takes an equity approach, it is very unlikely that dividends collected from positions will be able to support the distributions being paid. Therefore, IGA implements an option writing strategy to bridge that income gap. For instance, IGA uses an ATM (at-the-money) approach to generate option premiums and harness the volatility of the market. Based on market conditions, the fund can write options on anywhere between 50% and 100% of the notional value of its portfolio.

Unlike other funds that may write options on individual holdings, IGA writes options on market indices, which include the S&P 500 (SPX) or MSCI EAFE. This means that the fund is technically structured to capture the full upside growth of its positions over time. However, this structure can work against the fund during periods when the broad indices rally higher. This is because the option positions will be in the money, which IGA will have to pay out in cash to cover the option loss.

The fund doesn't have a set rebalancing schedule, but this may work in its favor. Since IGA is actively managed, the fund can adjust its holdings breakdown whenever it locates a position that is underperforming or no longer aligns with its eligibility requirements. When looking at the holdings of IGA, we can see that Alphabet (GOOG) is the largest holding at 3.14% of assets. This is followed by many other high-quality businesses, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Meta Platforms (META), Procter & Gamble (PG), and AbbVie (ABBV), just to name a few.

Underperformance Doesn't Justify the Yield

Despite IGA providing direct exposure to the highest quality companies in the world, the underlying structure means that capital appreciation isn't very likely. For instance, the inclusion of an option writing strategy means that upside growth is capped for the fund. Furthermore, the fund's high yield is quite aggressive for a traditional equity approach. There simply aren't many companies that produce a dividend yield high enough to support IGA's dividend yield of 10.7%. Therefore, the fund may frequently sell off the upside growth of its own positions, which limits share price growth.

When comparing IGA's performance against a more traditional index-tracking ETF, such as the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), we can see that IGA severely underperforms. IGA has a total return of 145.5% over the last decade, which is far below SPY's total return of 276% for the same time frame. Therefore, I want to emphasize that a high yield doesn't automatically translate to a high return over time.

Furthermore, IGA's primary allocation to financial companies can introduce some concentration risk for investors. For instance, the Fed has decided to leave interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, which leaves interest rates near the higher end of its ten-year range. A scenario where interest rates stay elevated can threaten financial companies as defaults begin to rise. Private credit and commercial real estate markets can deteriorate as borrowers struggle to keep up with the elevated cost of debt. Even if the banks don't default themselves, their stock price may be negatively impacted in anticipation of credit tightening. Since IGA relies on net realized gains to fuel its growth, the internal earnings of the fund may suffer.

Speaking of NAV, the long-term performance is an indication that something within IGA needs to be structurally improved. Since inception, IGA's share price has declined by nearly 53%, despite market indices racing to all-time highs over the last decade. If you were an IGA holder, you would have experienced your invested capital getting sliced in half, while traditional ETF investors would have grown their wealth effortlessly. Although the fund's total return since inception is attractive, I want investors to question whether or not these returns justify the continuous erosion of invested capital.

Dividend Accelerating NAV Erosion

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.085 per share, the current dividend yield now sits at 10.7%. Although the fund offers a high yield, IGA doesn't have the best track record of consistent payouts. Looking at the payout history below, we can see that the fund has a long track record of lowering payouts since its inception. However, an important aspect to call out is the shift to a monthly frequency during 2024, which was technically a slight bump in payouts.

Last Quarterly Payout: $0.1970 per share

Most Recent Monthly Payout: $0.085 per share

The downward-trending payout makes it very hard to recommend IGA for income investors seeking reliability. The underlying structure makes it very hard for the fund to maintain a consistent payout level, since the sustainability of payouts depends on positive market momentum conditions. More than a decade of dividend declines means that the long-term investors will be forced to manually reinvest a portion of their dividends back into the accumulation of shares in order to maintain a consistent annual income.

The combination of the fund's reliance on net realized gains and generous distributions means that the underlying NAV will struggle to grow. Even as market indices raced to new all-time highs during 2024, the most updated annual report shows us that the NAV still sits below the high of 2021. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $9.89 per share
  • 2021: $10.51 per share
  • 2022: $10.04 per share
  • 2023: $9.99 per share
  • 2024: $10.45 per share
  • 2025: $10.47 per share

When comparing the underlying NAV to other equity-focused closed-end funds, we can see that IGA has failed to keep up with the pace of average peer growth. So while the fund has continued to pay out income to shareholders, there are many periods where IGA has to eat away at its own assets during declines. Since the fund is primarily reliant on net realized gains, IGA has no way of providing protection against periods of prolonged market declines.

While the inclusion of an option writing strategy does provide a buffer, the fund will still decline during market pullbacks. In a scenario where market indices continue to trend downward for the remainder of 2026, IGA will be faced with the scenario of paying out a high dividend although it didn't actually earn enough income to support it.

However, this is quite a common dynamic for high-yield closed-end funds like this. For instance, the closest peer I could locate was the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (EXG). IGA shares many similarities with EXG, but the main differences come down to the portfolio allocation by region and sector. While IGA is most heavily allocated to financial companies, EXG chooses to focus on information technology companies.

EXG also offers a starting dividend yield of 8.9%. When comparing the performance of these two, we can see that EXG's share price only slightly outpaces IGA over the last ten years. When including all distributions paid, both funds share a pretty similar total return, but EXG slightly outperforms. An important difference is that EXG only writes options against 15% of its assets, which allows for more price upside participation.

On the bright side, the fund's reliance on net realized gains means that IGA tends to issue distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest available Section 19(a) notice indicates that 73% of the payouts YTD were classified as net realized long-term capital gains, which are qualified dividends and have the most favorable tax treatment for investors. This does reduce the tax burden for investors that want to utilize IGA outside of traditional tax-advantaged accounts. However, tax-efficient doesn't necessarily mean tax-free. Portions of the distributions classified as net investment income or short-term capital gains will have the greatest tax burden for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on IGA to sell. Although the fund sits at a reasonable price-to-NAV valuation, I believe the underlying structure doesn't align investors for success. The fund's dividend payout is a bit too generous, and the reliance on net realized gains means that the fund has no protection against market pullbacks. While the included option writing does introduce the ability to generate extra income, it also limits the growth potential over time. So while the fund offers a large double-digit yield, the fund has a history of eroding capital over time. So if we experience a prolonged downturn in the market, IGA's limited potential for price growth means that upside movements are limited. Therefore, IGA is likely to underperform even if market indices continue to race to new all-time highs.

data.title: Exchange Income: Sold Off Some Months Ago Due To Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note: Amounts are in Canadian Dollars unless noted

Few surprises are more pleasant than the ones where you invest in a company with the expectation of good income over time, but the company also then provides you with truly impressive capital appreciation.

This has been the case with Exchange Income Corporation (EIF:CA). This company has been on my coverage spectrum for a long, long time. Ever since I began investing, in fact. It was part of my original portfolio, where I considered it a solid core holding because it was a "monthly payor." My portfolio at the time was in part centered around "getting money every month" to pay for everything. This sort of "mailbox money" approach isn't a bad approach at all, even if I later found through trial and error that valuation-style approaches tend to suit me better.

Exchange Income is, aside from being an impressive payor, also a very impressive company fundamentally. The decline after my last article did not bother me in the least - I in fact, bought more, and it was a good time, given the upswing the company has seen. The price, as of the writing of this article, is now at $105/share.

As you can see from the returns below, something definitely happened from 2025 until now.

My position in this company was not small in the least. As I sold it, it was close to 3.5% of my portfolio due to both growth and reinvestment. I don't claim to have sold at the peak, but I managed to get a TSR of about 92%, which, for a company that provided me with very solid income over the years, was excellent.

My points in this article will have several underlying reasons. First off, I'll show you why the company has risen. It wasn't a bounce but a concerted upswing over time. Secondly, update my targets and show you where I'd invest.

To be clear, EIF is now at a very low yield. 2.5% is no longer attractive in itself because a bond/treasury gives over 4%. So what I'd need to get back into the company is a combined upside of capital appreciation and yield.

Let me show you what this would take.

Update After About 16 Months

So, maybe the first important question first. Is the company's upside in any way related to its earnings growth, and can it be justified by the underlying trends in the business?

I would answer that question with a resounding "no".

Since my last coverage, the company has gone from a 14-15x P/E to a P/E of above 27.5x. This would either mean a significant misevaluation on the part of the market (and me) previously, or it means that the market is being exuberant. I believe the latter is the fact here.

That is not to say the company has not done things to see capital appreciation. The financial performance of EIF since 2024 has truly been exceptional. In terms of earnings, the 2025A period saw a growth of over 20%, and in terms of operating cash flow, which is the metric I typically use for the company, things are up almost 70% for that period. In so seeing, there can be some understanding for the market's tendency to "go up" here.

But this does not mean that the company is suddenly any different from what it has been in the past. Its operational volatilities are still very much there. It still has the same tendency to miss negatively, 45% on a 1-year basis even with a 10% margin of error.

The airline, manufacturing, and transport industries, in which the company is involved, have not suddenly become less volatile or less risky to be in. Based on normalized multiples, the company is currently more than 20% overvalued to its 2025A operating cash flow multiples. I also believe that this level of OCF is not something that the company is likely to be able to maintain over time.

Why is that?

To understand this, we look at why the company outperformed in the first place. The reason we've seen such strong returns from this company is because of good M&A additions like Canadian North and 2025 having very good demand for both aerospace and the manufacturing segment. For basically every quarter in 2025, the company significantly outperformed its forecasts and expectations. This is what has driven premiumization.

The company also needs to be commended for consistently cutting down debt, using not only standard measures such as down payments but also strategic financial moves as well, such as redemption of convertibles.

The appeal of the company's niche markets, which I based my entire original thesis upon, also hasn't changed. It's still there, and the demand for things like remote aviation and medical services to far-away areas for a nation like Canada—all of these things are likely to continue. Specialized manufacturing adds more cyclicality to it, but because of its specialized nature, it's not as correlated as other types.

So with that being said, what's left for bears to focus on?

Unfortunately, what is left for bears is now a more valid thesis than the one 2 years ago. This largely has to do with valuation. The current valuation, whatever metric you choose to look at, leaves almost no room for error for the company.

It's fair to say that even the company's multiples, as you see above, look "odd". It's as though the company is not only taking into account the upside from 2025-2026, but from 2028-2030 as well, and estimating similar rates of growth - because that is what it would take to achieve the fair value multiples we're talking about here.

The problem is that while some estimates are expecting growth, others are forecasting lower levels, which would lead to a problem in how the company was valued. The current estimate for 2026 has the company's OCF dropping by 20%, with no more than 19% growth back in 2027E, followed by flattening out at a lower level in 2028E, which would average to an OCF growth rate of about 0.5% per year in total for the company. What is this expectation coming from?

A few factors. First, the company is heavily investing in new planes for the sake of fleet modernization. 12 new King Air planes have been ordered - delivered likely in mid-2026. Furthermore, its specialized manufacturing is very good at consuming capital for expansion. The new composite mat plant in the US is not expected to be close to operational until 2027 (part of the reason for the expected recovery) and will require significant investment with very little return.

Then there are macro concerns. We're talking about things like fuel price volatility, which impacts (directly) the company's margin in the core aviation operations.

I believe, to summarize, that the market is looking too closely at what made the company great as a relatively small operator in aviation, and I believe this somehow automatically translates to greatness in specialized manufacturing. I do not believe it does. I find support for my notion in highlighting that while the company's operating cash flow has increased from the 2014 levels, it always took macro or external factors, such as COVID-19, to provide a more material boost. I believe, therefore, that the recent spike in operating cash flow may indeed serve as a "rebase" for a somewhat higher "standard" level of cash flow, but it represents an outlier and will therefore contract/correct in the coming year.

I forecast with high conviction a significant drop in OCF - and I am not the only one to do so.

The forecasts I do not only have to do with factors described above but also segment-specific operating headwinds - such as EIF's window segment, which has seen project delays and reported efficiencies due to a significant reduction in demand, as well as significant short-term margin compression.

I therefore say there are no "magical" results, nor is this one, in what I believe is justified. With a revenue growth of 7% or so, but with a decline in OCF, I believe the upside if invested here is very low.

Valuation Not Attractive

By "deserve", I mean by what the company has ever historically achieved. At a current 20-year average to OCF, the company's expected returns if invested in at over $100 CAD are negative. Even with the dividends included, we're talking about 8.28% negative per year, or about 21% capital loss in 3 years. Without the dividend, that increases to almost 30%, and that includes expected dividend growth.

In order to see a market-beating upside here, forecasting EIF at over 9x P/OCF is not enough. Even at 10x P/OCF, which is more than double the multiple where I bought my shares originally, your annualized rate of return comes to less than 5% per year. That is inclusive of dividends, by the way.

We have some "soft" indicators as well of the overvaluation. Usually, insider trading in the company is quite limited, but recently, a holder sold about 3,000 shares above $101/share, which represented a cut in his 9,000 stake of about 33%. This in itself is, of course, not enough to make a company unattractive, but the various indicators "stack up" in my view, to where I don't see the appeal as all that great for the company.

There are other indicators we can look at for the company's expensive nature. Its P/E sits currently close to 34x. That is more than 30% higher than the TSX composite, with averages at about 20-21x. Canadian industrials, which is what the company partially is, sit at about 24x - again, much lower than EIF. Also, and perhaps the most jarring, is the airline average, which is 10x, but 3x higher for the company.

While I agree that its specialized operations do deserve a premium, I do not believe this sort of premium is what is deserved by the company. I therefore consider the company to be significantly overvalued.

My previous PT for the company was about $65/share for EIF's native ticker. Translating my previous valuation logic to 2025 results would increase that to $77/share - the problem is that the upswing is only momentary (in my view), which makes the need to increase it significantly lower. I therefore increase my PT for the company to $70/share, which comes to about 7x OCF for the next 2026E period, which also comes to the company's 20-year average (or close to it).

Bought any more expensively, I see the potential for negative results as too high.

This comes to an EIZF target of $50/share, also increased.

As of this article, I have completely sold my entire position in EIF and consider the company overvalued.

Thesis

This is a small operator with a big upside at the right price. Fundamentals are solid, and I like their operations and their niche. At an attractive price, and for the right investor, this is a definite "Buy." But at a $70 PT for EIF, upgraded from previous targets and the current valuation, this company does not merit significant attention due to overvaluation.

My stance is a "HOLD", and I believe the company is above its fair-value price target as of April 2026.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of $70/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Microsoft: The Market Is Forcing Me To Buy This Bargain (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) is that type of company that you know deserves a premium valuation, and you know you will probably never pay a cheap price on the stock. But that is no longer true.

I have been following Microsoft for a few years now, and I have never seen the stock with multiples this compressed, and this is not just an empirical perception. Looking at the fwd P/E, it is practically at its historical minimum, and even if we analyze the 10-year history of the last twelve months (LTM) P/E, the multiple is also very close to the all-time low. It is worth mentioning that I don't even consider the LTM P/E that accurate, since Microsoft's growth is substantial and is above its avg.

And this is already a point to be considered. Microsoft is indeed a mature company, but its growth, both top line and EBITDA, is higher than its average of the last 10 years. There is a lot of opportunity, a lot of innovation, but the market seems to insist on seeing the glass “half empty.”

It is true that there are uncertainties in the case, but as the long-term thesis remains not only intact but even stronger with this "huge margin of safety" I am upgrading the rating of Microsoft to a strong buy.

Microsoft’s Risks: OpenAI, CapEx, Xbox

Before starting to discuss the positives of Microsoft, I need to say that the case really has some uncertainties. In fact, the title of my last article on MSFT was “Microsoft: Solid Fundamentals, Rising Uncertainties -Why I Remain Cautiously Bullish.” Basically, I said that although I was optimistic about Microsoft, I had concerns about competition regarding AI and cloud, and mainly, I believed that Microsoft possessed a “bad” part of revenue, which was Xbox and Windows.

I don't like this division much because of the uncertainty. It is far from being a recurring revenue like the others, not to mention that today the environment is very dynamic for gaming, so platforms like Xbox can really lose relevance if the strategy is not assertive, not only to Sony (SONY) platforms like PlayStation, which would be the most obvious, but there are also threats from Steam and other alternatives.

The market in general has a fear regarding Cloud CapEx, but investments in gaming are also a concern. Of course, not in the same magnitude, but I think an analogy can be drawn here. Billions were spent on acquisitions like Activision Blizzard and Bethesda, with the intention of expanding the catalog of IPs for Game Pass, making this subscription something truly relevant and a main pillar of this niche for Microsoft. But it is difficult to say how much this is really important. Xbox revenue is not in such a good momentum (see image below), and new strategies emerge all the time, studios being closed, shifts in focus (console, handheld, subscription, and so on), and all this seems to me to make the scenario more uncertain. Will Microsoft really manage to have a compelling ROIC on these investments? More importantly, can this revenue be considered something healthy? I mean, a revenue that will grow in the mid-single digits on average over the next few years, and with a minimally satisfactory margin.

These are valid concerns, but the market ends up ignoring them by focusing on what changes the case much more, which is Cloud. And as for Cloud, as much as I am more optimistic than the market, as I will discuss in the next section, I also have my concerns. Competition is one of them, since other large companies like Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) and Amazon (AMZN) are in this game, besides neoclouds like Nebius (NBIS), but I believe my main fear currently is regarding OpenAI (OPENAI).

I see some analysts and people in the market highlighting that OpenAI was a good investment because Microsoft multiplied the invested capital, but going a bit beyond that, I think we can also see OpenAI as a liability. Not exactly a liability in accounting terms, but I say it because it ends up being an uncertainty for Azure.

Currently, Azure has an extensive backlog and very strong demand, and that is what drives the gigantic CapEx, to try to mitigate this scarcity and capture this profitability. But a good part of the backlog, and I say, a good part for real, is OpenAI. About 45%, to be more specific.

If you follow the “battle” of LLMs closely, you probably imagine what I will say here. Anthropic is very hot, since its model focused on enterprise is showing to be a success, more profitable (at least initially) than the focus on small subscriptions like OpenAI did. Alphabet with Gemini also challenges the “leadership” of ChatGPT at all times, with models that frequently are better in some aspects (and every few months the leader in performance/efficiency changes).

And as this model of OpenAI is still not profitable at all—actually, it is quite unprofitable, since the overwhelming majority uses only the free version—the visibility of the next years is quite harmed. We do not know if the CPM (cost per mille/million) token will fall enough to the point of a subscription of ~$20 being profitable; we do not know if OpenAI will be able to continue growing (top line and bottom line) for the next 10 years or if Anthropic/Gemini and related will continue stealing its market share, and this goes against Microsoft’s CapEx.

If OpenAI demand does not continue growing, maybe in a few years with all this investment in Data Centers, there will be idle capacity, and the ROIC of this current CapEx may not be able to reach the internal estimates of the company. Even worse, if there is a problem with OpenAI, maybe this will be a catalyst for a drop in demand, which would be another problem for the current CapEx and for the Cloud division in general.

But I Am Bullish With Cloud/AI

However, even in this scenario, I remain bullish with Microsoft’s cloud division. Actually, I have been bullish with Cloud for some years, and it was even one of the reasons that I started my Amazon position, because cloud growth has already been a “secular trend” for some years. Even without counting on AI, other trends, such as ML (machine learning), digitalization, and more and more data being used and generated, were already important tailwinds.

And maybe it sounds strange saying that I am optimistic with Cloud right after I say that I have concerns with the “over” dependence on OpenAI, but I will explain.

The main reason for this is that I am still optimistic in the long term. Even if 45% of the backlog ends up having some problem (maybe it does not grow as much or anything like that), the CapEx of cloud is basically infrastructure. That is, Microsoft is developing new and solid infrastructure to capture a growth that will not occur only in 2026 or 2027; it is a revenue that can occur in the next decades.

In some moments maybe there will be ups and downs of price, demand, etc. However, in the long run, it is, in general, a quite solid demand. I think the easiest way to bring this to reality is thinking in tokens. Today many people already consume tokens. I mean, when making a question to ChatGPT or to Gemini, or even a simple search on Google, all of these are "token demand."

Next year, will people consume more or less tokens? The answer for me is clear: more. Each year that passes, more people will know LLMs, AI agents. Personal assistants in smartphones (think Siri) will start to have this type of feature, and consequently, “tons” of tokens will be used daily.

And for me, enterprise is an even more obvious trend. Each year, companies from different sectors will create agents. And many of these new agents will consume many tokens; some will consume tokens 24/7. All of this demands computing power.

Of course, there still exist other uncertainties that affect the level of profitability of these Data Centers like electricity costs, depreciation and replacement/maintenance costs, and so on. But in general, it still seems to me a very positive trend and a demand that, in the long term, is reliable and growing.

A Word About Next Quarter

Mainly because of the market, I believe that the 2 main factors to keep an eye on in the next earnings release (which occurs at the end of this month; see image below) are the Cloud segment and the CapEx. If Microsoft manages to maintain healthy margins even with this high CapEx/high CapEx estimates, maintain the Azure growth very high, and show that the backlog is still solid, these are points that can make the market stop punishing Microsoft for “betting” so much on this segment.

A more personal point, but that matters. It is also interesting to look at the “less sexy” segments (Xbox/Windows). It is something that matters little for the market, but maybe it impacts at some moment, to see if the growth is already a bit more accelerated, if there is any catalyst (like AI PCs, to see how initiatives like the increase of Game Pass are maturing, etc.).

The expectations are for an EPS of ~$4.06, slightly lower QoQ but with a solid growth of 17%, so even though the market cares a lot about beat and miss, I believe that even if MSFT manages to deliver exactly $4.06, it will already be a very solid result (and I believe it will manage).

Bottom Line: Compounder at a Bargain Price

In short, Microsoft is at a price level that already prices in uncertainties and a strong disbelief from the market. Note that I dwelt quite a bit on the Cloud division, but bear in mind that Microsoft is not just that. Microsoft still has a part of its revenue from subscription services, which is a must-have for enterprises and ends up anchoring this compounding status for the long-term financials.

So to summarize, here we are talking about a company with a wide moat, delivering a gigantic growth opportunity, a top-line growth that is already being delivered (~15% YoY), and an EPS growing at even more accelerated levels, and all this for a fwd P/E of 22x, well below its 5y avg of 31x.

For this reason, I am upgrading to strong buy, and even though I already have a position in the other 2 large hyperscalers (Amazon and Alphabet), I should initiate a position in Microsoft in the coming weeks (depending on price and other personal factors).

data.title: Rare Earths: Fledgling Rare Earths Recycling Sector Offers Non-China Supply
data.description:
Rare earth elements have come under increasing focus owing to their criticality to defense equipment manufacturing and clean technology. China has monopolized this supply chain for over two decades and has not shied away from using it for geopolitical leverage, forcing others to think of a diversification strategy. This is Part 3 of a 5-part series in which Platts News explores the strategies under play and the challenges they face.

Western nations and their allies are making major investments in supply chains for recycled rare earths, but closing the gap with China comes with significant challenges.

China dominates global rare earth supply chains, which are essential for defense applications, electric vehicles and other advanced technologies. The country accounted for 60% of global mined production and 91% of global refining capacity for key rare earths in 2024, according to data from the International Energy Agency.

China has leveraged its dominance to develop a robust recycling supply chain that extracts more rare earths from production scrap and electronic waste. However, major investments from the US, EU and others aim to bolster rare earths recycling capacity and overcome economic challenges.

"Recycling is important for future supply [outside of China], but the key issue right now is setting up the logistics and establishing those feedstock networks," Chris Berry, founder and president of the House Mountain Partners advisory firm, told Platts, part of S&P Global Energy. "Without that, I don't see how you go through the process of building a large enough scalable plant to make it economically feasible."

China's lead

China's long-term position as the world's largest producer of rare earths has enabled the development of mature, efficient systems in which plants convert scrap into powder that can easily be recycled to extract more rare earth elements, said David Merriman, research director at critical materials consulting firm Project Blue.

Western nations, by comparison, lag far behind in their rare earth recycling capabilities.

"The capacity for rare earth recycling outside of China is minuscule compared to that within the Chinese market currently," Merriman told Platts.

"There's a number of reasons for that, including feedstock availability and accessing production scrap," Merriman said. "The industry outside of the Chinese market has had to focus on end-of-life material that is difficult to source, dismantle and process. It's made it difficult to make magnets recycling and recovery economic outside of China."

Berry highlighted tech giant Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) $500 million deal with California-based MP Materials Corp. (MP) as the most significant recycled rare earths supply partnership outside of China.
